1.o: Much is to be learned by comparing one part of Scripture with another. We here behold Solomon returning from the broken and empty cisterns of the world, to the Fountain of living water; recording his own folly and shame, the bitterness of his disappointment, and the lessons he had learned. Those that have taken warning to turn and live, should warn others not to go on and die. He does not merely say all things are vain, but that they are vanity. VANITY OF VANITIES, ALL IS VANITY. This is the text of the preacher's sermon, of which in this book he never loses sight. If this world, in its present state, were all, it would not be worth living for; and the wealth and pleasure of this world, if we had ever so much, are not enough to make us happy. What profit has a man of all his labour? All he gets by it will not supply the wants of the soul, nor satisfy its desires; will not atone for the sins of the soul, nor hinder the loss of it: what profit will the wealth of the world be to the soul in death, in judgment, or in the everlasting state?
1.m:
We learn a lot by looking at different parts of the Bible together. Here, we see Solomon coming back from places that gave him nothing good—empty pools—to the true source of life. He tells about his mistakes, his sadness, and what he learned. People who realize their mistake and start living right should tell others not to keep going down the wrong path. 

He doesn't just say everything is useless, but that it’s all vanity. "VANITY OF VANITIES, ALL IS VANITY." This is the main message of the preacher's talk, which he keeps repeating. If this world was all there is, it wouldn’t be worth living in. The money and fun of this world won’t make you truly happy, even if you had lots of them. 

What do people gain from working hard? Everything they get won’t fill their deepest needs or stop their souls from being lost. It won’t help their souls avoid punishment or save them in the end. How useful will the riches of this world be after death, during judgment, or forever?

1.o: All things change, and never rest. Man, after all his labour, is no nearer finding rest than the sun, the wind, or the current of the river. His soul will find no rest, if he has it not from God. The senses are soon tired, yet still craving what is untried.
1.m: Everything changes and nothing stays the same. After all his hard work, man finds no more rest than the sun, the wind, or the flow of a river. Without God, his soul won’t find peace. Our senses get worn out quickly, always wanting what we haven’t tried yet.

1.o: Men's hearts and their corruptions are the same now as in former times; their desires, and pursuits, and complaints, still the same. This should take us from expecting happiness in the creature, and quicken us to seek eternal blessings. How many things and persons in Solomon's day were thought very great, yet there is no remembrance of them now!
1.m:
People's hearts and their bad tendencies are just like they were long ago. Their desires, actions, and complaints haven't changed. 

This should remind us not to look for happiness in worldly things. It should encourage us to search for eternal blessings instead.

Many people and things in Solomon’s time were considered very important back then. But today, nobody remembers them anymore.

1.o: Solomon tried all things, and found them vanity. He found his searches after knowledge weariness, not only to the flesh, but to the mind. The more he saw of the works done under the sun, the more he saw their vanity; and the sight often vexed his spirit. He could neither gain that satisfaction to himself, nor do that good to others, which he expected. Even the pursuit of knowledge and wisdom discovered man's wickedness and misery; so that the more he knew, the more he saw cause to lament and mourn. Let us learn to hate and fear sin, the cause of all this vanity and misery; to value Christ; to seek rest in the knowledge, love, and service of the Saviour.
1.m:
Solomon tried everything and found it meaningless. He found searching for knowledge tiring, both physically and mentally. The more he looked at what people did on Earth, the more he saw how pointless it was. This often upset him. 

He couldn't find the happiness for himself or help others as much as he hoped. Pursuing knowledge and wisdom showed him how bad and sad life could be. So the more he learned, the more he felt sorry.

Let’s learn to hate and fear sin, which causes all this emptiness and sadness. We should treasure Christ and look for peace by knowing, loving, and serving the Savior.

2.o: Solomon soon found mirth and pleasure to be vanity. What does noisy, flashy mirth towards making a man happy? The manifold devices of men's hearts, to get satisfaction from the world, and their changing from one thing to another, are like the restlessness of a man in a fever. Perceiving it was folly to give himself to wine, he next tried the costly amusements of princes. The poor, when they read such a description, are ready to feel discontent. But the remedy against all such feelings is in the estimate of it all by the owner himself. All was vanity and vexation of spirit: and the same things would yield the same result to us, as to Solomon. Having food and raiment, let us therewith be content. His wisdom remained with him; a strong understanding, with great human knowledge. But every earthly pleasure, when unconnected with better blessings, leaves the mind as eager and unsatisfied as before. Happiness arises not from the situation in which we are placed. It is only through Jesus Christ that final blessedness can be attained.
2.m:
Solomon quickly realized that fun and enjoyment were meaningless. How much does loud, fancy fun really help someone be happy? People try many different ways to find happiness in the world, moving from one thing to another, just like a sick person tossing and turning. Solomon saw that drinking too much wasn’t smart, so he then tried expensive entertainment like rich people do. Poor people might feel jealous when they read about these things. 

But the real lesson comes from what Solomon thought about them. He said it was all empty and frustrating. These same things would leave anyone feeling the same way. We should be happy with having enough food and clothes. Solomon still had his wisdom—a sharp mind full of human knowledge. 

Yet, without deeper joy, even those pleasures left him wanting more. True happiness doesn’t come from where we live or what we have. Only through Jesus Christ can we find true peace and joy.

2.o: Solomon found that knowledge and prudence were preferable to ignorance and folly, though human wisdom and knowledge will not make a man happy. The most learned of men, who dies a stranger to Christ Jesus, will perish equally with the most ignorant; and what good can commendations on earth do to the body in the grave, or the soul in hell? And the spirits of just men made perfect cannot want them. So that if this were all, we might be led to hate our life, as it is all vanity and vexation of spirit.
2.m: Solomon saw that knowing things was better than being ignorant, even if having lots of knowledge won't make someone truly happy. The smartest person who doesn’t know Christ will suffer just as much as an uneducated person. Praise from people here on Earth won’t help a dead body in the ground or save a soul in hell. Righteous souls in heaven don’t need such praise. So, if this was everything, we might end up hating our lives because it’s all empty and frustrating.

2.o: Our hearts are very loth to quit their expectations of great things from the creature; but Solomon came to this at length. The world is a vale of tears, even to those that have much of it. See what fools they are, who make themselves drudges to the world, which affords a man nothing better than subsistence for the body. And the utmost he can attain in this respect is to allow himself a sober, cheerful use thereof, according to his rank and condition. But we must enjoy good in our labour; we must use those things to make us diligent and cheerful in worldly business. And this is the gift of God. Riches are a blessing or a curse to a man, according as he has, or has not, a heart to make a good use of them. To those that are accepted of the Lord, he gives joy and satisfaction in the knowledge and love of him. But to the sinner he allots labour, sorrow, vanity, and vexation, in seeking a worldly portion, which yet afterwards comes into better hands. Let the sinner seriously consider his latter end. To seek a lasting portion in the love of Christ and the blessings it bestows, is the only way to true and satisfying enjoyment even of this present world.
2.m:
Our hearts don't want to give up hoping for big things from the world. But Solomon learned that the world is full of sadness, even for people who have a lot. People are foolish if they work hard just to get enough to live on. 

The most someone can do is use what they have in a sensible, happy way based on their situation. We should enjoy what we have while working. This kind of enjoyment is a gift from God. 

Riches can be a blessing or a curse depending on whether someone uses them well. For those God accepts, He gives joy and peace through knowing and loving Him. But sinners get trouble, sorrow, and frustration trying to find wealth, which often ends up belonging to others anyway. 

Sinners need to think about what will happen later. The only real happiness comes from finding a permanent part in the love of Christ and His gifts. That’s the key to truly enjoying life now too.

3.o: To expect unchanging happiness in a changing world, must end in disappointment. To bring ourselves to our state in life, is our duty and wisdom in this world. God's whole plan for the government of the world will be found altogether wise, just, and good. Then let us seize the favourable opportunity for every good purpose and work. The time to die is fast approaching. Thus labour and sorrow fill the world. This is given us, that we may always have something to do; none were sent into the world to be idle.
3.m: Expecting constant happiness in a constantly changing world leads to disappointment. It’s our duty and smart to accept the situation we’re in here on Earth. God’s plans for running the world are all wise, fair, and good. So let’s take advantage of every chance to do good things. Death is coming soon for everyone. That’s why hard work and struggles fill our lives. We are given tasks to keep busy because nobody was put on Earth to be lazy.

3.o: Every thing is as God made it; not as it appears to us. We have the world so much in our hearts, are so taken up with thoughts and cares of worldly things, that we have neither time nor spirit to see God's hand in them. The world has not only gained possession of the heart, but has formed thoughts against the beauty of God's works. We mistake if we think we were born for ourselves; no, it is our business to do good in this life, which is short and uncertain; we have but little time to be doing good, therefore we should redeem time. Satisfaction with Divine Providence, is having faith that all things work together for good to them that love him. God doeth all, that men should fear before him. The world, as it has been, is, and will be. There has no change befallen us, nor has any temptation by it taken us, but such as is common to men.
3.m:
Everything is how God made it, not how it seems to us. We get so caught up in thinking about worldly things that we don't notice God's hand in them. The world takes over our hearts and makes us forget the beauty of God’s creations. 

We're wrong if we think we exist just for ourselves. Our real purpose is to do good while we’re here, because life is short and unpredictable. We need to use our time wisely since we have so little of it to help others. Being satisfied with what God provides means trusting that he'll work everything out for those who love him.

God does everything to show people how great he is. The world stays the same—past, present, and future. Nothing new happens to us that hasn’t happened to other people. The temptations we face are normal ones that everyone else faces too.

3.o: Without the fear of the Lord, man is but vanity; set that aside, and judges will not use their power well. And there is another Judge that stands before the door. With God there is a time for the redressing of grievances, though as yet we see it not. Solomon seems to express his wish that men might perceive, that by choosing this world as their portion, they brought themselves to a level with the beasts, without being free, as they are, from present vexations and a future account. Both return to the dust from whence they were taken. What little reason have we to be proud of our bodies, or bodily accomplishments! But as none can fully comprehend, so few consider properly, the difference between the rational soul of man, and the spirit or life of the beast. The spirit of man goes upward, to be judged, and is then fixed in an unchangeable state of happiness or misery. It is as certain that the spirit of the beast goes downward to the earth; it perishes at death. Surely their case is lamentable, the height of whose hopes and wishes is, that they may die like beasts. Let our inquiry be, how an eternity of existence may be to us an eternity of enjoyment? To answer this, is the grand design of revelation. Jesus is revealed as the Son of God, and the Hope of sinners.
3.m:
Without the fear of the Lord, people are just empty. Without that fear, leaders won't do what's right. There is also a judge waiting outside. 

God has a time to fix problems, even if we don’t see it now. Solomon thought that people who choose this world over God’s kingdom are like animals. Animals aren’t troubled now, nor do they face judgment later. 

Both humans and animals go back to dust after they die. We shouldn’t be proud of our bodies or skills. Few really think about the big difference between human souls and animal spirits. 

The human spirit rises up to be judged and then stays in a place of endless joy or pain. An animal’s spirit goes down to the ground and ends at death. Their hope is sad—just to die like an animal. 

We should ask how we can enjoy forever. That’s what God’s message aims to show. Jesus came as the Son of God and the hope for all sinners.

4.o: It grieved Solomon to see might prevail against right. Wherever we turn, we see melancholy proofs of the wickedness and misery of mankind, who try to create trouble to themselves and to each other. Being thus hardly used, men are tempted to hate and despise life. But a good man, though badly off while in this world, cannot have cause to wish he had never been born, since he is glorifying the Lord, even in the fires, and will be happy at last, for ever happy. Ungodly men have most cause to wish the continuance of life with all its vexations, as a far more miserable condition awaits them if they die in their sins. If human and worldly things were our chief good, not to exist would be preferable to life, considering the various oppressions here below.
4.m:
It upset Solomon to see power win over justice. Everywhere we look, we see sad examples of people causing trouble for themselves and others. Because of this bad treatment, some people feel like hating and disliking life. 

But a good person, even if they suffer in this world, has no reason to regret being born. They honor God, even in hard times, and will eventually find happiness forever. Unbelievers have the strongest reason to keep living through all their troubles because an even worse fate awaits them if they die without changing. 

If earthly things were our main goal, not existing would seem better than living, given all the oppression here on Earth.

4.o: Solomon notices the sources of trouble peculiar to well-doers, and includes all who labour with diligence, and whose efforts are crowned with success. They often become great and prosperous, but this excites envy and opposition. Others, seeing the vexations of an active course, foolishly expect more satisfaction in sloth and idleness. But idleness is a sin that is its own punishment. Let us by honest industry lay hold on the handful, that we may not want necessaries, but not grasp at both hands full, which would only create vexation of spirit. Moderate pains and gains do best.
4.m:
Solomon points out problems that successful people face. He talks about those who work hard and succeed. 

These people often become rich and famous, which makes others jealous and mean to them. Some see the troubles of working hard and think being lazy would be better. But laziness just causes more problems for itself.

We should work honestly to earn enough to live comfortably without needing too much. Too much greed leads to stress. Working moderately brings the most happiness.

4.o: Frequently, the more men have, the more they would have; and on this they are so intent, that they get no enjoyment from what they have. Selfishness is the cause of this evil. A selfish man cares for nobody; there is none to take care of but himself, yet he will scarcely allow necessary rest to himself, and the people he employs. He never thinks he has enough. He has enough for his calling, for his family, but he has not enough for his eyes. Many are so set upon the world, that in pursuit of it they bereave themselves, not only of the favour of God and eternal life, but of the pleasures of this life. The distant relations or strangers who inherit such a man's wealth, never thank him. Covetousness gathers strength by time and habit; men tottering on the brink of the grave, grow more grasping and griping. Alas, and how often do we see men professing to be followers of Him, who, "though he was rich, for our sakes became poor," anxiously scraping money together and holding it fast, excusing themselves by common-place talking about the necessity of care, and the danger of extravagance!
4.m:
Often, the more people have, the more they want. They focus so much on getting more that they don’t enjoy what they already have. This happens because they are selfish. A selfish person only cares about themselves. They think they’re the only one who needs anything, even though they hardly give themselves proper rest or let their workers rest either. 

They always feel like they need more. They might have enough for their work and family, but they still think they don’t have enough. Some people chase after riches so much that they lose God’s favor and eternal life. They miss out on enjoying life too. When these greedy people die, their far-off relatives or strangers inherit their money. Those people won’t even thank them.

Being greedy gets worse over time. As older people near death, they become even greedier. It’s sad to see some Christians, who know Jesus gave up his riches for us, still hoard money. They say things like “we need to be careful” or “it’s dangerous to spend too much,” to excuse their greed.

4.o: Surely he has more satisfaction in life, who labours hard to maintain those he loves, than the miser has in his toil. In all things union tends to success and safety, but above all, the union of Christians. They assist each other by encouragement, or friendly reproof. They warm each other's hearts while they converse together of the love of Christ, or join in singing his praises. Then let us improve our opportunities of Christian fellowship. In these things all is not vanity, though there will be some alloy as long as we are under the sun. Where two are closely joined in holy love and fellowship, Christ will by his Spirit come to them; then there is a threefold cord.
4.m:
Certainly someone gets more joy from working hard to take care of loved ones than a greedy person does from their efforts. 

Unity helps in everything, especially among Christians. They help each other by giving support or gentle correction. Talking about Jesus' love or singing songs about him warms their hearts. We should use every chance we get for Christian friendship. 

Even if there are some problems, these moments aren’t empty. When two people are close in faith and fellowship, Christ comes to them through his Spirit. That makes a strong bond, like a rope with three strands.

4.o: People are never long easy and satisfied; they are fond of changes. This is no new thing. Princes see themselves slighted by those they have studied to oblige; this is vanity and vexation of spirit. But the willing servants of the Lord Jesus, our King, rejoice in him alone, and they will love Him more and more to all eternity.
4.m:
People are never happy for long. They always want things to be different. This isn't anything new. 

Princes feel hurt when people ignore them even though the princes try to please everyone. This causes sadness and frustration.

But the followers of Jesus, our King, find joy only in him. And they will keep loving him forever.

5.o: Address thyself to the worship of God, and take time to compose thyself for it. Keep thy thoughts from roving and wandering: keep thy affections from running out toward wrong objects. We should avoid vain repetitions; copious prayers are not here condemned, but those that are unmeaning. How often our wandering thoughts render attendance on Divine ordinances little better than the sacrifice of fools! Many words and hasty ones, used in prayer, show folly in the heart, low thoughts of God, and careless thoughts of our own souls.
5.m:
Prepare yourself to worship God. Focus your mind and don't let your thoughts wander. Keep your feelings focused on the right things.

Don’t repeat yourself uselessly. Long prayers aren’t bad, but empty ones are. Our minds often drift during worship, making it pointless. Using many quick, random words in prayer shows you don’t think much about God or care deeply about your soul.

5.o: When a person made engagements rashly, he suffered his mouth to cause his flesh to sin. The case supposes a man coming to the priest, and pretending that his vow was made rashly, and that it would be wrong to fulfil it. Such mockery of God would bring the Divine displeasure, which might blast what was thus unduly kept. We are to keep down the fear of man. Set God before thee; then, if thou seest the oppression of the poor, thou wilt not find fault with Divine Providence; nor think the worse of the institution of magistracy, when thou seest the ends of it thus perverted; nor of religion, when thou seest it will not secure men from suffering wrong. But though oppressors may be secure, God will reckon for all.
5.m:
When someone made promises carelessly, their words led them to do things that were sinful. This situation involves a person going to the priest and saying their vow was made by mistake and should not have been made at all. Pretending this way would upset God, who could punish what was wrongly kept. 

We need to avoid being afraid of people. Instead, we should focus on God. When you see unfair treatment of those less fortunate, don’t blame God’s plan. Don’t doubt the importance of leaders just because they sometimes fail. And don’t lose faith in religion just because bad things still happen.

Even if oppressors seem safe now, God will hold them accountable one day.

5.o: The goodness of Providence is more equally distributed than appears to a careless observer. The king needs the common things of life, and the poor share them; they relish their morsel better than he does his luxuries. There are bodily desires which silver itself will not satisfy, much less will worldly abundance satisfy spiritual desires. The more men have, the better house they must keep, the more servants they must employ, the more guests they must entertain, and the more they will have hanging on them. The sleep of the labourer is sweet, not only because he is tired, but because he has little care to break his sleep. The sleep of the diligent Christian, and his long sleep, are sweet; having spent himself and his time in the service of God, he can cheerfully repose in God as his Rest. But those who have every thing else, often fail to secure a good night's sleep; their abundance breaks their rest. Riches do hurt, and draw away the heart from God and duty. Men do hurt with their riches, not only gratifying their own lusts, but oppressing others, and dealing hardly with them. They will see that they have laboured for the wind, when, at death, they find the profit of their labour is all gone like the wind, they know not whither. How ill the covetous worldling bears the calamities of human life! He does not sorrow to repentance, but is angry at the providence of God, angry at all about him; which doubles his affliction.
5.m:
Providence’s kindness spreads evenly if we look closely enough. Kings need basic things just like everyone else, and the poor enjoy these basics too. A simple meal tastes better than fancy food to someone who truly appreciates it. Money cannot satisfy our deepest desires, let alone fulfill our spiritual needs. The richer you get, the bigger home you need, the more staff you hire, and the more guests you entertain. This means more people depend on you.

A hard worker sleeps well because they’re tired and don’t worry much. A diligent Christian sleeps soundly after serving God faithfully. But rich people often struggle to sleep well because their worries keep them awake.

Riches can harm us by pulling our hearts away from God and our duties. People use wealth to indulge themselves and mistreat others. At the end, they realize their efforts were wasted—like chasing the wind. Their gains vanish without trace.

Wealthy people often react poorly to life’s troubles. Instead of feeling sorry and changing, they get angry at God and those around them. This makes their problems worse.

5.o: Life is God's gift. We must not view our calling as a drudgery, but take pleasure in the calling where God puts us. A cheerful spirit is a great blessing; it makes employments easy, and afflictions light. Having made a proper use of riches, a man will remember the days of his past life with pleasure. The manner in which Solomon refers to God as the Giver, both of life and its enjoyments, shows they ought to be received and to be used, consistently with his will, and to his glory. Let this passage recommend to all the kind words of the merciful Redeemer, "Labour not for the meat that perisheth, but for that meat which endureth unto everlasting life." Christ is the Bread of life, the only food of the soul. All are invited to partake of this heavenly provision.
5.m:
Life comes from God. We should see our work as a joy, not a burden. Being happy about what we do makes tasks easier and helps us handle problems better. When someone uses their wealth well, they look back on their life happily. 

Solomon reminds us that God gives us both life and its pleasures. So we should accept these gifts and use them in ways that please God. This verse encourages everyone to listen to Jesus' kind words: “Don’t work just for food that spoils, but for the food that lasts forever.” Jesus is the Bread of Life, the only true nourishment for our souls. Everyone is welcome to share in this eternal meal.

6.o: A man often has all he needs for outward enjoyment; yet the Lord leaves him so to covetousness or evil dispositions, that he makes no good or comfortable use of what he has. By one means or other his possessions come to strangers; this is vanity, and an evil disease. A numerous family was a matter of fond desire and of high honour among the Hebrews; and long life is the desire of mankind in general. Even with these additions a man may not be able to enjoy his riches, family, and life. Such a man, in his passage through life, seems to have been born for no end or use. And he who has entered on life only for one moment, to quit it the next, has a preferable lot to him who has lived long, but only to suffer.
6.m:
A person might have everything they need for pleasure. But God lets them stay greedy or mean, so they don’t enjoy what they have. Their things go to strangers somehow. This is pointless and a bad sickness. Having many children was very important and respected by the Hebrews. Long life is what most people want. Yet even with those blessings, someone might still not enjoy their wealth, family, or time alive. 

Such a person seems to have wasted their whole life. Someone who lives just for a short while might actually have it better than someone who lives longer but suffers.

6.o: A little will serve to sustain us comfortably, and a great deal can do no more. The desires of the soul find nothing in the wealth of the world to give satisfaction. The poor man has comfort as well as the richest, and is under no real disadvantage. We cannot say, Better is the sight of the eyes than the resting of the soul in God; for it is better to live by faith in things to come, than to live by sense, which dwells only upon present things. Our lot is appointed. We have what pleases God, and let that please us. The greatest possessions and honours cannot set us above the common events of human life. Seeing that the things men pursue on earth increase vanities, what is man the better for his worldly devices? Our life upon earth is to be reckoned by days. It is fleeting and uncertain, and with little in it to be fond of, or to be depended on. Let us return to God, trust in his mercy through Jesus Christ, and submit to his will. Then soon shall we glide through this vexatious world, and find ourselves in that happy place, where there is fulness of joy and pleasures for evermore.
6.m:
A little bit can keep us comfortable, and having a lot doesn’t really help much more. Wealth doesn’t satisfy our deepest desires. Poor people can be just as happy as rich ones, and aren’t at a true disadvantage. 

We shouldn't think that seeing nice things is better than finding peace in God. Faith in future blessings is better than living only for what we see now. 

Whatever we have was given to us by God. We should be content with that. Having lots of money or honor won’t protect us from life’s usual troubles. 

Since all the things people chase after don’t really matter, what good does it do them? Life on Earth is short and uncertain. There isn’t much to love about it or depend on. 

Let’s turn back to God, trust in His mercy through Jesus, and accept His plans for us. This way, we’ll quickly get through this troublesome world and enter the joyful, perfect place He prepared for us.

7.o: Reputation for piety and honesty is more desirable than all the wealth and pleasure in this world. It will do more good to go to a funeral than to a feast. We may lawfully go to both, as there is occasion; our Saviour both feasted at the wedding of his friend in Cana, and wept at the grave of his friend in Bethany. But, considering how apt we are to be vain and indulge the flesh, it is best to go to the house of mourning, to learn the end of man as to this world. Seriousness is better than mirth and jollity. That is best for us which is best for our souls, though it be unpleasing to sense. It is better to have our corruptions mortified by the rebuke of the wise, than to have them gratified by the song of fools. The laughter of a fool is soon gone, the end of his mirth is heaviness.
7.m: Having a good reputation for being honest and kind is better than having lots of money and fun. Going to a funeral can help you more than going to a party. You can go to both if needed—Jesus went to both weddings and funerals. But since people often get carried away with fun and pleasure, it’s better to go to a sad event and think about life. Being serious is better than just laughing and having fun. What’s best for your soul is what matters most, even if it’s not fun. It’s better to have your bad habits corrected by wise people than to let them grow from foolish jokes. A fool’s laughter doesn’t last long, and their joy turns to sadness quickly.

7.o: The event of our trials and difficulties is often better than at first we thought. Surely it is better to be patient in spirit, than to be proud and hasty. Be not soon angry, nor quick in resenting an affront. Be not long angry; though anger may come into the bosom of a wise man, it passes through it as a way-faring man; it dwells only in the bosom of fools. It is folly to cry out upon the badness of our times, when we have more reason to cry out for the badness of our own hearts; and even in these times we enjoy many mercies. It is folly to cry up the goodness of former times; as if former ages had not the like things to complain of that we have: this arises from discontent, and aptness to quarrel with God himself.
7.m:
Our troubles often turn out better than we expect at first. Being patient is much better than being proud and quick to get upset. Don’t get angry quickly over small offenses. Don’t stay angry for long. Even smart people might feel angry sometimes, but they don’t hold onto it. Fools keep their anger around longer.

It’s silly to blame everything on how bad the world is now. We should instead focus on fixing our own bad attitudes. And even in tough times, we still have many good things to be thankful for.

It’s also foolish to think past times were perfect. People back then faced problems too. This kind of thinking shows we’re unhappy and ready to argue with God.

7.o: Wisdom is as good as an inheritance, yea better. It shelters from the storms and scorching heat of trouble. Wealth will not lengthen out the natural life; but true wisdom will give spiritual life, and strengthen men for services under their sufferings. Let us look upon the disposal of our condition as the work of God, and at last all will appear to have been for the best. In acts of righteousness, be not carried into heats or passions, no, not by a zeal for God. Be not conceited of thine own abilities; nor find fault with every thing, nor busy thyself in other men's matters. Many who will not be wrought upon by the fear of God, and the dread of hell, will avoid sins which ruin their health and estate, and expose to public justice. But those that truly fear God, have but one end to serve, therefore act steadily. If we say we have not sinned, we deceive ourselves. Every true believer is ready to say, God be merciful to me a sinner. Forget not at the same time, that personal righteousness, walking in newness of life, is the only real evidence of an interest by faith in the righteousness of the Redeemer. Wisdom teaches us not to be quick in resenting affronts. Be not desirous to know what people say; if they speak well of thee, it will feed thy pride, if ill, it will stir up thy passion. See that thou approve thyself to God and thine own conscience, and then heed not what men say of thee; it is easier to pass by twenty affronts than to avenge one. When any harm is done to us, examine whether we have not done as bad to others.
7.m:
Wisdom is just as valuable as an inheritance, maybe even more so. It protects you from troubles and hardships. Money won't extend your lifespan, but true wisdom gives you spiritual life and helps you endure suffering. Look at your situation as God’s doing, and everything will seem to have turned out for the best.

In acts of righteousness, don’t get too emotional or angry, even if you’re zealous about God. Don’t think too highly of yourself, and don’t criticize everything around you. Stay out of other people’s business.

Many people avoid sins that hurt their health and lead them to legal trouble because they want to stay safe. But those who truly fear God do what’s right without worrying about different motives.

If we claim we haven’t sinned, we’re fooling ourselves. A true believer admits, “God, be merciful to me, I am a sinner.”

At the same time, remember that living right and changing your ways shows that you trust in Jesus’ righteousness. 

Wisdom tells us not to jump to conclusions about insults. Avoid wanting to know what others say about you. If they praise you, it might puff you up, and if they criticize you, it could upset you. Focus on pleasing God and your own conscience instead of caring what others think.

When someone hurts you, check if you’ve ever done the same to others.

7.o: Solomon, in his search into the nature and reason of things, had been miserably deluded. But he here speaks with godly sorrow. He alone who constantly aims to please God, can expect to escape; the careless sinner probably will fall to rise no more. He now discovered more than ever the evil of the great sin of which he had been guilty, the loving many strange women, I Kin. 11:1. A woman thoroughly upright and godly, he had not found. How was he likely to find such a one among those he had collected? If any of them had been well disposed, their situation would tend to render them all nearly of the same character. He here warns others against the sins into which he had been betrayed. Many a godly man can with thankfulness acknowledge that he has found a prudent, virtuous woman in the wife of his bosom; but those men who have gone in Solomon's track, cannot expect to find one. He traces up all the streams of actual transgression to the fountain. It is clear that man is corrupted and revolted, and not as he was made. It is lamentable that man, whom God made upright, has found out so many ways to render himself wicked and miserable. Let us bless Him for Jesus Christ, and seek his grace, that we may be numbered with his chosen people.
7.m:
Solomon searched deeply into the nature of things but got misled badly. Now, he talks about it with true regret. Only someone who always tries to please God can avoid trouble. Careless sinners usually keep falling without getting back up. 

He realized even more clearly how wrong it was to love many foreign women, as he did in 1 Kings 11:1. He couldn't find an honest, godly woman among them. If any were good, their circumstances would still make them act poorly. 

Now, he warns others about the mistakes he made. Some godly men have found wise, virtuous wives. But those who followed Solomon’s path won’t find one. 

He looks at where bad actions start. People become corrupt and do wrong, not as God intended. It’s sad that humans, who were meant to be good, find so many ways to be wicked and unhappy. 

Let’s praise God for Jesus Christ and ask for His help so we can join His chosen people.

8.o: None of the rich, the powerful, the honourable, or the accomplished of the sons of men, are so excellent, useful, or happy, as the wise man. Who else can interpret the words of God, or teach aright from his truths and dispensations? What madness must it be for weak and dependent creatures to rebel against the Almighty! What numbers form wrong judgments, and bring misery on themselves, in this life and that to come!
8.m: No one among the rich, powerful, or successful people is as great, helpful, or happy as the wise person. Only the wise can explain God’s words correctly and teach others about His truth. It’s foolish for weak humans to go against the all-powerful God. Many people judge things wrongly and cause themselves trouble now and in the future.

8.o: God has, in wisdom, kept away from us the knowledge of future events, that we may be always ready for changes. We must all die, no flight or hiding-place can save us, nor are there any weapons of effectual resistance. Ninety thousand die every day, upwards of sixty every minute, and one every moment. How solemn the thought! Oh that men were wise, that they understood these things, that they would consider their latter end! The believer alone is prepared to meet the solemn summons. Wickedness, by which men often escape human justice, cannot secure from death.
8.m:
God has wisely kept us from knowing what will happen in the future so that we stay ready for changes. Everyone will die someday. Running away or hiding won't help. There’s nothing we can do to stop it. 

Ninety thousand people die each day. That means more than sixty people die every minute, and someone dies every single moment. It’s a serious thing to think about. I wish people could see how important it is to think about the end of life.

Only those who believe in God are truly ready for death. Evil actions might let some people avoid being caught by humans, but it won’t protect them from dying.

8.o: Solomon observed, that many a time one man rules over another to his hurt, and that prosperity hardens them in their wickedness. Sinners herein deceive themselves. Vengeance comes slowly, but it comes surely. A good man's days have some substance; he lives to a good purpose: a wicked man's days are all as a shadow, empty and worthless. Let us pray that we may view eternal things as near, real, and all-important.
8.m: Solomon noticed that often one person rules over another for harm. Prosperity makes evil people even worse. Sinners fool themselves. Revenge might take time, but it always comes. Good people live meaningful lives. Wicked people just waste their days. Let’s ask God to help us see eternal things as close, real, and very important.

8.o: Faith alone can establish the heart in this mixed scene, where the righteous often suffer, and the wicked prosper. Solomon commended joy, and holy security of mind, arising from confidence in God, because a man has no better thing under the sun, though a good man has much better things above the sun, than soberly and thankfully to use the things of this life according to his rank. He would not have us try to give a reason for what God does. But, leaving the Lord to clear up all difficulties in his own time, we may cheerfully enjoy the comforts, and bear up under the trials of life; while peace of conscience and joy in the Holy Ghost will abide in us through all outward changes, and when flesh and heart shall fail.
8.m:
Faith alone can keep our hearts steady in a world where good people sometimes face hardships and bad people seem to do well. 

Solomon said it’s best to be happy and at peace because you trust God. This is important since there’s nothing better here on earth for a good person than using their blessings wisely. A good person has even more wonderful things waiting for them after death.

We shouldn’t try to explain everything God does. Instead, we should leave those questions to him and enjoy the good times and handle the hard times with joy. When we do this, we’ll always have inner peace and happiness from the Holy Spirit, no matter what happens around us.

9.o: We are not to think our searching into the word or works of God useless, because we cannot explain all difficulties. We may learn many things good for ourselves and useful to others. But man cannot always decide who are objects of God's special love, or under his wrath; and God will certainly put a difference between the precious and the vile, in the other world. The difference as to present happiness, arises from the inward supports and consolations the righteous enjoy, and the benefit they derive from varied trials and mercies. As far as the sons of men are left to themselves, their hearts are full of evil; and prosperity in sin, causes them even to set God at defiance by daring wickedness. Though, on this side death, the righteous and the wicked may often seem to fare alike, on the other side there will be a vast difference between them.
9.m:
We shouldn't think that studying God’s word or actions is useless just because we can’t solve all puzzles. We can still learn lots of helpful things for ourselves and others. 

But people can't always tell who God loves especially or punishes. God will surely show the difference between those he values and those he doesn’t in the afterlife. 

The difference in happiness now comes from the inner peace and joy the good people have, along with the benefits they get from different tests and blessings. 

When humans act alone without God, their hearts are filled with bad thoughts. Success in doing wrong makes them dare to challenge God openly. 

Even if the good and bad people look similar here on earth, in the next life, there will be a big difference between them.

9.o: The most despicable living man's state, is preferable to that of the most noble who have died impenitent. Solomon exhorts the wise and pious to cheerful confidence in God, whatever their condition in life. The meanest morsel, coming from their Father's love, in answer to prayer, will have a peculiar relish. Not that we may set our hearts upon the delights of sense, but what God has given us we may use with wisdom. The joy here described, is the gladness of heart that springs from a sense of the Divine favour. This is the world of service, that to come is the world of recompence. All in their stations, may find some work to do. And above all, sinners have the salvation of their souls to seek after, believers have to prove their faith, adorn the gospel, glorify God, and serve their generation.
9.m:
Even the worst person alive is better off than the greatest person who dies without changing their ways. 

Solomon tells smart and religious people to trust God and be happy, no matter what their situation is. Even a small meal from God’s love, received through prayer, tastes extra special. We shouldn’t focus on earthly pleasures, but we can enjoy what God gives us wisely. 

This happiness comes from feeling God’s favor. Our current life is for serving God, while the next life is for rewards. Everyone can find tasks to do in their role. Sinners need to seek their soul’s salvation, and believers should test their faith, honor God, and help others.

9.o: Men's success seldom equals their expectations. We must use means, but not trust to them: if we succeed, we must give God the praise; if crossed, we must submit to his will. Those who put off the great concerns of their souls, are caught in Satan's net, which he baits with some worldly object, for which they reject or neglect the gospel, and go on in sin till they suddenly fall into destruction.
9.m:
Success often doesn't match what people expect. We should try things, but not rely too much on them. If we do well, we thank God. If we don’t, we accept what He decides.

People who wait too long to deal with important spiritual matters get trapped by Satan. He tricks them with worldly things. They ignore the gospel and keep sinning until they suddenly face ruin.

9.o: A man may, by his wisdom, bring to pass that which he could never do by his strength. If God be for us, who can be against us, or stand before us? Solomon observes the power of wisdom, though it may labour under outward disadvantages. How forcible are right words! But wise and good men must often content themselves with the satisfaction of having done good, or, at least, endeavoured to do it, when they cannot do the good they would, nor have the praise they should. How many of the good gifts, both of nature and Providence, does one sinner destroy and make waste! He who destroys his own soul destroys much good. One sinner may draw many into his destroying ways. See who are the friends and enemies of a kingdom or a family, if one saint does much good, and one sinner destroys much good.
9.m:
A person can achieve things through their wisdom that they couldn't do with just their strength. If God is on our side, who can stand against us?

Solomon noticed how powerful wisdom can be, even when someone faces challenges from outside. Right words are very effective.

Wise and good people sometimes have to be happy knowing they tried their best to do good, even if they didn’t get the results they wanted or the credit they deserved.

How many good things—both natural talents and blessings from God—are wasted by one bad person! Someone who ruins their own life causes a lot of harm.

One sinful person might lead others down a destructive path too. To see who helps or hurts a country or a family, look at those who do great good and those who cause a lot of damage.

10.o: Those especially who make a profession of religion, should keep from all appearances of evil. A wise man has great advantage over a fool, who is always at a loss when he has anything to do. Sin is the reproach of sinners, wherever they go, and shows their folly.
10.m: People who claim to follow religion should avoid anything that looks bad. Wise people have an easier time than fools when dealing with things. Sin embarrasses those who commit it everywhere they go and shows how foolish they are.

10.o: Solomon appears to caution men not to seek redress in a hasty manner, nor to yield to pride and revenge. Do not, in a passion, quit thy post of duty; wait awhile, and thou wilt find that yielding pacifies great offences. Men are not preferred according to their merit. And those are often most forward to offer help, who are least aware of the difficulties, or the consequences. The same remark is applied to the church, or the body of Christ, that all the members should have the same care one for another.
10.m:
Solomon seems to be telling people not to act quickly out of anger or pride. Don't leave your duties in a fit of rage. Wait a bit, and you'll see that being patient helps calm big problems.

People aren't always chosen based on what they deserve. Often, those who rush to help don't realize the challenges or results of their actions.

This advice applies to the church too. All members should care for each other equally.

10.o: There is a practice in the East, of charming serpents by music. The babbler's tongue is an unruly evil, full of deadly poison; and contradiction only makes it the more violent. We must find the way to keep him gentle. But by rash, unprincipled, or slanderous talk, he brings open or secret vengeance upon himself. Would we duly consider our own ignorance as to future events, it would cut off many idle words which we foolishly multiply. Fools toil a great deal to no purpose. They do not understand the plainest things, such as the entrance into a great city. But it is the excellency of the way to the heavenly city, that it is a high-way, in which the simplest wayfaring men shall not err, Is. 25:8. But sinful folly makes men miss that only way to happiness.
10.m:
In some places, people use music to control snakes. A gossip’s tongue is wild and dangerous, filled with harmful lies. Arguing just makes them angrier. 

We need to find ways to calm them down. But if someone talks nonsense or spreads rumors, they bring trouble on themselves—either openly or secretly. 

If we think about how little we know about what will happen next, we’d stop saying so many silly things. Fools work hard for nothing. They don’t even understand simple stuff, like entering a big city. 

But the path to heaven is different. It’s clear and easy, like a highway where anyone—even those who aren’t very smart—can follow without getting lost, Isaiah 25:8. Yet sin and foolishness lead people away from the only true path to real happiness.

10.o: The happiness of a land depends on the character of its rulers. The people cannot be happy when their princes are childish, and lovers of pleasure. Slothfulness is of ill consequence both to private and public affairs. Money, of itself, will neither feed nor clothe, though it answers the occasions of this present life, as what is to be had, may generally be had for money. But the soul, as it is not redeemed, so it is not maintained with corruptible things, as silver and gold. God sees what men do, and hears what they say in secret; and, when he pleases, brings it to light by strange and unsuspected ways. If there be hazard in secret thoughts and whispers against earthly rulers, what must be the peril from every deed, word, or thought of rebellion against the King of kings, and Lord of lords! He seeth in secret. His ear is ever open. Sinner! curse not THIS KING in thy inmost thought. Your curses cannot affect Him; but his curse, coming down upon you, will sink you to the lowest hell.
10.m:
The happiness of a country depends on the leaders who run it. People won't be happy if their rulers act like children and love having fun all the time. Being lazy hurts both personal lives and the whole community. 

Money helps buy things we need, but it doesn’t feed us or clothe us. It’s useful for daily needs because most things cost money. However, our souls aren't saved or kept healthy by money or valuable metals.

God knows everything we do and hear what we whisper about secretly. When he wants to, he reveals hidden things through surprising methods. 

If it's dangerous to think bad thoughts about human rulers, imagine how risky it is to rebel against the King of all kings and Lord of all lords. He sees everything done in secret. He always listens too. 

Sinner, don’t curse this King even in your mind. Curses can’t hurt him, but his punishment will send you to the deepest part of hell.

11.o: Solomon presses the rich to do good to others. Give freely, though it may seem thrown away and lost. Give to many. Excuse not thyself with the good thou hast done, from the good thou hast further to do. It is not lost, but well laid out. We have reason to expect evil, for we are born to trouble; it is wisdom to do good in the day of prosperity. Riches cannot profit us, if we do not benefit others. Every man must labour to be a blessing to that place where the providence of God casts him. Wherever we are, we may find good work to do, if we have but hearts to do it. If we magnify every little difficulty, start objections, and fancy hardships, we shall never go on, much less go through with our work. Winds and clouds of tribulation are, in God's hands, designed to try us. God's work shall agree with his word, whether we see it or not. And we may well trust God to provide for us, without our anxious, disquieting cares. Be not weary in well-doing, for in due season, in God's time, you shall reap, Gal. 6:9.
11.m:
Solomon urges the wealthy to help others generously. Even if giving seems like losing money, give freely anyway. Help lots of people. Don’t think you’ve already done enough just because you’ve helped some. What you give isn’t really lost—it’s wisely spent.

We should expect troubles since life brings them. But it’s smart to do good during times of plenty. Wealth won’t help us unless we use it to help others. Everyone should aim to be a blessing wherever God puts them. 

There’s always good work to do if we want to do it. Don’t let small problems stop us or imagine things will be hard. Trials and difficulties come from God to test us. 

God’s plans match what he says, even if we don’t see it. Trust God to take care of you, so don’t worry too much. Keep doing good deeds. At the right time, which is God’s timing, you’ll get rewards, Galatians 6:9 tells us.

11.o: Life is sweet to bad men, because they have their portion in this life; it is sweet to good men, because it is the time of preparation for a better; it is sweet to all. Here is a caution to think of death, even when life is most sweet. Solomon makes an effecting address to young persons. They would desire opportunity to pursue every pleasure. Then follow your desires, but be assured that God will call you into judgment. How many give loose to every appetite, and rush into every vicious pleasure! But God registers every one of their sinful thoughts and desires, their idle words and wicked words. If they would avoid remorse and terror, if they would have hope and comfort on a dying bed, if they would escape misery here and hereafter, let them remember the vanity of youthful pleasures. That Solomon means to condemn the pleasures of sin is evident. His object is to draw the young to purer and more lasting joys. This is not the language of one grudging youthful pleasures, because he can no longer partake of them; but of one who has, by a miracle of mercy, been brought back in safety. He would persuade the young from trying a course whence so few return. If the young would live a life of true happiness, if they would secure happiness hereafter, let them remember their Creator in the days of their youth.
11.m:
Life is sweet to bad people because they enjoy what they get in this world. It’s sweet to good people too, because it’s a time to prepare for something better. Life is sweet for everyone.

There’s a warning to always think about death, even when life feels really good. Solomon talks to young people directly. Young people often want to do whatever pleases them. So go ahead and follow those desires, but know that God will judge you someday.

Many people give in to every temptation and chase after all kinds of wrong pleasures. But God writes down everything—every sinful thought, every idle word, every evil deed. To avoid feeling guilty and scared at the end of life, to feel hopeful instead, to avoid suffering now and forever—to do these things, young people should realize that youthful pleasures don’t last.

Solomon isn’t condemning youthful pleasures just because he can’t enjoy them anymore. Instead, he wants young people to choose better, more lasting joys. He’s speaking from experience, having been saved from a dangerous path himself. 

If young people want real happiness now and later, they need to remember their Creator while they’re still young.

12.o: We should remember our sins against our Creator, repent, and seek forgiveness. We should remember our duties, and set about them, looking to him for grace and strength. This should be done early, while the body is strong, and the spirits active. When a man has the pain of reviewing a misspent life, his not having given up sin and worldly vanities till he is forced to say, I have no pleasure in them, renders his sincerity very questionable. Then follows a figurative description of old age and its infirmities, which has some difficulties; but the meaning is plain, to show how uncomfortable, generally, the days of old age are. As the four verses, 2-5, are a figurative description of the infirmities that usually accompany old age, Eccl. 12:6 notices the circumstances which take place in the hour of death. If sin had not entered into the world, these infirmities would not have been known. Surely then the aged should reflect on the evil of sin.
12.m:
We need to think about the sins we've committed against God, feel sorry for them, and ask for forgiveness. We should also remember our responsibilities and work hard at doing them, asking God for help along the way. 

This should start early in life, when we still have energy and enthusiasm. If someone only stops sinning and giving up worldly pleasures when they're too weak to enjoy them anymore, their honesty might be doubted.

Next, there’s a picture painted of what happens in old age—how people often become weaker and less able to do things. The message here is clear: old age is usually pretty tough.

Verses 2 through 5 describe the usual problems that come with getting older. In verse 6, Ecclesiastes talks about what happens right before dying.

If sin hadn’t entered the world, none of these troubles would exist. So, older people especially should think about how bad sin really is.

12.o: Solomon repeats his text, VANITY OF VANITIES, ALL IS VANITY. These are the words of one that could speak by dear-bought experience of the vanity of the world, which can do nothing to ease men of the burden of sin. As he considered the worth of souls, he gave good heed to what he spake and wrote; words of truth will always be acceptable words. The truths of God are as goads to such as are dull and draw back, and nails to such as are wandering and draw aside; means to establish the heart, that we may never sit loose to our duty, nor be taken from it. The Shepherd of Israel is the Giver of inspired wisdom. Teachers and guides all receive their communications from him. The title is applied in Scripture to the Lord Jesus Christ, the Son of God. The prophets sought diligently, what, or what manner of time, the Spirit of Christ in them did signify, when it testified beforehand the sufferings of Christ, and the glory that should follow. To write many books was not suited to the shortness of human life, and would be weariness to the writer, and to the reader; and then was much more so to both than it is now. All things would be vanity and vexation, except they led to this conclusion, That to fear God, and keep his commandments, is the whole of man. The fear of God includes in it all the affections of the soul towards him, which are produced by the Holy Spirit. There may be terror where there is no love, nay, where there is hatred. But this is different from the gracious fear of God, as the feelings of an affectionate child. The fear of God, is often put for the whole of true religion in the heart, and includes its practical results in the life. Let us attend to the one thing needful, and now come to him as a merciful Saviour, who will soon come as an almighty Judge, when he will bring to light the things of darkness, and manifest the counsels of all hearts. Why does God record in his word, that ALL IS VANITY, but to keep us from deceiving ourselves to our ruin? He makes our duty to be our interest. May it be graven in all our hearts. Fear God, and keep his commandments, for this is all that concerns man.
12.m:
Solomon repeats his main message: "Vanity of vanities, all is vanity." This comes from someone who learned firsthand how empty worldly things are because they cannot take away the weight of sin. When Solomon thought about the value of souls, he spoke and wrote carefully. Truthful words are always welcome.

God’s truths act like goads—tools used to guide stubborn animals—and like nails that hold things firmly in place. They help steady our hearts so we don’t drift away from doing what’s right. The Shepherd of Israel gives wise thoughts. Teachers get their ideas from him. 

The term “Shepherd of Israel” refers to Jesus Christ, the Son of God. Prophets worked hard to understand what the Spirit of Christ meant when it talked about Jesus’ suffering and future glory. Writing too many books isn't helpful since life is short, and it tires both writers and readers. It’s even worse now.

Everything becomes meaningless unless it leads to fearing God and obeying his commands. Fearing God covers all the emotions we have toward him, created by the Holy Spirit. People can feel scared without loving God, even hating him. But this is different from the kind of fear that shows love, like a child feels toward a parent.

Fearing God means having true faith in our hearts and living according to it. We should focus on what truly matters and turn to Jesus as our Savior now, before he returns as judge. God writes that "all is vanity" to stop us from fooling ourselves. He wants us to see that following him is best for us. Let’s remember this and engrave it deeply in our hearts.

Fear God and follow his commands, because that is what matters most for people.
